title: Pit-wave and rival-relative context features
summary: Competition-aware F1 strategy research argues that ignoring rivals materially changes optimal pit timing, and sports analytics literature repeatedly finds context-normalized features stronger than absolute KPIs.
rationale: Competition-aware F1 strategy research argues that ignoring rivals materially changes optimal pit timing, and sports analytics literature repeatedly finds context-normalized features stronger than absolute KPIs. A next-lap pit often happens as a response to an undercut threat or a field pit wave.
implementation_hint: Create same-race/current-lap aggregates available at decision time: count/share of other cars pitting now, count in nearby position bands pitting now, driver lap-time and degradation percentile within race-lap and within compound, and interaction terms like `slow_relative_pace x nearby_pit_wave`. Keep all aggregates contemporaneous, never future-looking.
expected_effect: Should add signal for reactive stops that the current solo-driver heuristics miss, especially around undercut/cover-stop behavior.
risk: Using race-lap aggregates is valid only if they are based on current-lap observables; any accidental use of future laps or target-derived labels will inflate CV.
